notes: Dates for each H-1B cap cycle. USCIS announces the exact registration window each January–February; values flagged with todo:true are based on the usual pattern and MUST be verified against the official USCIS cap-season announcement at the source URL before each season. Registration/selection counts come from the USCIS post-season data release.

cycles.label: FY 2027 (registered March 2026, start Oct 1 2026)
cycles.todoNote: TODO: verify the FY2027 registration window, selection date, and post-season registration/selection counts against uscis.gov. Dates below follow the typical March pattern; the registration fee rose to $215 per beneficiary effective the FY2026 season — confirm it still applies.

cycles.label: FY 2026 (registered March 2025, start Oct 1 2025)
cycles.todoNote: TODO: verify FY2026 registration count (~343,981 eligible registrations) and selection count (~120,141) against the official USCIS FY2026 cap-season figures.

phases.label: Electronic registration window
whatToDo: Your employer (or their attorney) creates/uses a USCIS organizational account and submits an electronic registration for you with your passport details.
Each beneficiary needs only one registration per employer; the $215 fee is paid per registration.
Double-check the spelling of your name and passport number — they must match your passport exactly for the eventual petition.
pitfalls: Since 2024 the lottery is beneficiary-centric: registering through multiple employers no longer increases your odds, and fraudulent multiple registrations are flagged and denied.
Registrations submitted after the window closes are not accepted — there is no grace period.

phases.label: Initial selection / lottery results
phases.typical: By March 31 (usually within a few days of the window closing)
whatToDo: Check the employer's USCIS organizational account — selected registrations show the status "Selected."
If selected, your employer can begin preparing the full I-129 petition and your supporting documents.
If not selected, your status shows "Submitted" — you remain eligible if USCIS runs a second round.
pitfalls: Master's-cap math: the regular 65,000 lottery is run first across ALL eligible registrations; advanced-degree registrations not selected there get a second draw for the extra 20,000 — so a US master's improves, but does not guarantee, your odds.
Selection is not approval — it only grants the right to file a petition.

phases.label: Petition filing window (I-129)
phases.typical: Opens April 1; at least a 90-day window to file
whatToDo: Your employer files Form I-129 with the selection notice, an LCA certified by the DOL, your degree evaluation, and job/specialty-occupation evidence.
Earliest receipt date is April 1; the filing window stays open at least 90 days from your selection notice.
Gather your I-94, passport, prior visa/status documents, diplomas, and transcripts now so the petition is not delayed.
pitfalls: Missing the filing window forfeits the selection — the slot is not held for next year.
A defective or uncertified LCA is a common cause of rejection; the LCA must be certified before the I-129 is filed.

phases.label: Premium vs. regular processing
phases.typical: Decided at filing (premium can be added later)
whatToDo: Decide whether to pay for premium processing (Form I-907) for a 15-business-day adjudication commitment, versus standard processing that can take months.
Premium is useful if you need a decision before a status expiry, a change-of-employer deadline, or visa stamping travel.
Premium can be added after filing if circumstances change.
pitfalls: Premium processing guarantees a response time, not an approval — USCIS can issue an RFE or denial within the window.
The premium fee changes periodically; confirm the current I-907 fee before filing.

phases.label: USCIS adjudication
whatToDo: Track the case status with the receipt number on egov.uscis.gov.
Keep your employer informed of any travel or status changes while the case is pending.
If you are changing status from F-1/OPT, understand cap-gap rules that can extend work authorization to October 1.
pitfalls: Regular processing times vary widely by service center; don't book international travel around an unconfirmed approval date.
If you are in cap-gap, leaving the US before approval can break the cap-gap extension.

phases.label: Possible Request for Evidence (RFE)
phases.typical: If issued, usually with a ~60–87 day response deadline
whatToDo: If USCIS issues an RFE, work with your employer/attorney to respond fully before the stated deadline.
Common RFE topics: specialty-occupation/degree relevance, employer-employee relationship, and LCA wage level.
Submit all requested evidence in a single, well-organized response.
pitfalls: Missing the RFE deadline almost always results in denial — calendar it the day it arrives.
A weak first response wastes your one chance; over-document rather than under-document.

phases.label: Possible second selection round
phases.typical: July–August, only if USCIS does not reach the cap
whatToDo: If the cap isn't met from the first round, USCIS runs additional selections from the existing registration pool — you do not re-register.
Keep your documents ready in case your "Submitted" registration is upgraded to "Selected."
Watch the employer's organizational account and USCIS announcements through the summer.
pitfalls: A second round is not guaranteed every year — in high-demand years the cap is reached in the first round.
If selected in a later round, your filing window opens then (not April 1), so act quickly.

phases.label: Earliest H-1B start date
phases.typical: October 1 — start of the federal fiscal year
whatToDo: Cap-subject H-1B employment can begin no earlier than October 1 of the selection year.
If you're on F-1 cap-gap, your OPT work authorization bridges to October 1 once the petition is timely filed/approved.
If consular processing abroad, schedule visa stamping so you can enter and begin on or after October 1.
pitfalls: Starting cap-subject work before October 1 is not permitted (cap-exempt employers are the exception).
Plan stamping appointments early — consular wait times in India can be long.

capExempt.summary: Cap-exempt employers — universities and their affiliated nonprofits, nonprofit research organizations, and government research organizations — are NOT subject to the annual H-1B cap or the lottery.
points: No March registration and no lottery: a cap-exempt petition can be filed at any time of year.
There is no October 1 start restriction — employment can begin as soon as the petition is approved (or on the requested start date).
If you later move to a cap-subject employer, you will generally need to go through the cap/lottery at that point.

stats.feeNote: USCIS raised the H-1B registration fee to $215 per beneficiary starting the FY2026 season — verify the current amount each cycle.
